macro_segments.title: Introduction to Megillat Ta'anit and the Debate over Its Status Post-Churban

micro_segments.title: The scroll of fasts and the basic dispute: Rav and Rabbi Chanina versus Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi
Explanation of Megillat Ta'anit as an ancient Second Temple document listing non-fasting days
The five fast days and their reversal from fast to rejoicing during the Beit Hamikdash era
Comparison of how fast days revert versus how rabbinic holidays retain their status

macro_segments.title: The Incident in Lod: Public Rejection of a Fast on Chanukah and the Distinction Between Holiday Status and Fasting Prohibition

micro_segments.title: An incident in Lod where a fast was declared on Chanukah, contradicting Megillat Ta'anit
Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua's public demonstration rejecting the fast through bathing and haircuts
Tosafot's explanation that Chanukah can retain its status as a holiday (with candle-lighting) even if Megillat Ta'anit is annulled
The distinction between the halakhic status of Chanukah as a yom tov for religious purposes and its status regarding fasting prohibition

macro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Defense of Chanukah's Retention and Abaye's Challenge

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef argues that Chanukah is different because it has a mitzvah of candle-lighting
Abaye challenges whether we should preserve Chanukah if we're annulling Megillat Ta'anit for other days
Rav Yosef's refined answer: Chanukah retains status because the miracle was very public (pirsumei nisa)
Rashi's perspective: Chanukah is retained not because of objective factors but because it is too entrenched in society to reverse

macro_segments.title: The Third of Tishrei: Removing God's Name from Documents and the Hasmonean Decree

micro_segments.title: The Megillat Ta'anit entry for the 3rd of Tishrei regarding the removal of God's name from legal documents
Historical context: the Greek decree against mentioning God's name and the Hasmonean reversal allowing it
The concern that if God's name appears in secular documents they will be discarded, leading the sages to prohibit writing God's name on them
Making the 3rd of Tishrei a yom tov to commemorate the successful change in the law regarding God's name in documents

macro_segments.title: Logical Problem: Why Add the Third of Tishrei if Tzom Gedaliah Already Became a Holiday

micro_segments.title: The Gemara questions how the third of Tishrei could have been added during the Beit Hamikdash era when Tzom Gedaliah already became a holiday
The Gemara's resolution: the purpose was to extend the prohibition to the day before via the rabbinic strengthening of rabbinic holidays
The distinction that biblical holidays (like Tzom Gedaliah based on Zechariah) do not extend their prohibition to preceding days

macro_segments.title: Post-Churban Evidence: The 28th of Adar and Yehuda ben Shamua's Successful Protest Against Roman Persecution

micro_segments.title: The Megillat Ta'anit entry for the 28th of Adar regarding a Roman decree against learning Torah, brit milah, and Shabbat
Yehuda ben Shamua and his colleagues seeking advice from a matronit (noblewoman) who influenced Roman officials
The nocturnal protest and the public declaration appealing to their shared patriarchal and maternal ancestry with Esau
The successful reversal of the Roman decree and the establishment of the 28th of Adar as a yom tov

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Resolution: Post-Churban Usage Proves Megillat Ta'anit Was Not Annulled

micro_segments.title: The Gemara objects that adding holidays post-Churban (under Yehuda ben Shamua, post-destruction) suggests Megillat Ta'anit remained active
The resolution that both events (3rd of Tishrei and 28th of Adar additions) occurred during the Beit Hamikdash era, not afterward
The Gemara presents a tannaitic dispute: Rabbi Meir holds the days retain status even post-Churban; Rabbi Yose holds they lose status without the Beit Hamikdash
The halacha decides that most days from Megillat Ta'anit were annulled except for Chanukah and Purim, which retained lasting significance

macro_segments.title: Returning to the Mishnah: Sending Shlichim for Elul, Tishrei, Kislev, and Adar

micro_segments.title: Elul: Shlichim are sent in case Elul is only 29 days so people know when Rosh Hashanah begins
Tishrei: Shlichim are sent again to ensure the months are established in their proper time (tikkun hamo'adot)
The Gemara's question: if Elul is always 29 days (attested from Ezra onward), why send shlichim again for Tishrei?
The Gemara's answer: although Elul being 29 days is constant practice, it remains theoretically possible to make it 30 days if necessary for proper calendar coordination

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Tikkun HaMo'adot: Prioritizing Accurate Holiday Dates Over Consistency in Elul

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's principle: it is better for Rosh Hashanah to be observed on the wrong date than to compromise the accuracy of other holidays
The reason: tikkun hamo'adot (fixing the holidays) is the paramount concern, requiring acceptance of moon-sighting testimony even if it makes Elul 30 days
The implication that months are lunar-based and tied to actual moon appearance, not fixed calendar cycles

macro_segments.title: Chanukah and Purim: Sending Shlichim for Kislev and Adar

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah mentions sending shlichim for Kislev (for Chanukah) and Adar (for Purim)
The Gemara notes that the Mishnah does not mention sending shlichim for Adar Sheni in a leap year, suggesting a debate
Rabbinic opinion (unnamed majority) holds that all mitzvot of Adar Sheni can be performed in Adar Rishon, so shlichim are not needed for Adar Sheni
Rabbi's opinion holds that mitzvot specific to Adar Sheni cannot be performed in Adar Rishon, requiring shlichim to be sent for Adar Sheni

macro_segments.title: The Length of Adar Rishon in a Leap Year: Varying Opinions on Fixed vs. Variable Months

micro_segments.title: The Gemara debates whether shlichim are needed for Adar Sheni based on whether Adar Rishon has a fixed or variable length
If Adar Rishon is always 30 days, one can calculate when Adar Sheni begins without shlichim; if it varies, shlichim are necessary
The Tannaic tradition from Rabbi Simai citing Chaggai, Zechariah, and Malachi: both Adars can be full (30), lacking (29), or one full and one lacking
The opinion attributed to Rav: one Adar is always full (30) and one always lacking (29), unless there is explicit testimony that Adar Sheni was established in its proper time

macro_segments.title: The Standard Rule for Adar: The Month Preceding Nissan is Always 29 Days

micro_segments.title: A ruling sent to Mar Ukva stating that Adar immediately preceding Nissan is always 29 days

macro_segments.title: Why Violate Shabbat to Testify About Nissan and Tishrei When the Dates May Be Predictable

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah permits violating Shabbat to testify about two months: Nissan and Tishrei, for the sake of fixing the holidays
Rav Nachman's challenge: if Adar before Nissan is always 29 days, why violate Shabbat to testify about Nissan?
The Gemara's response: there is a mitzvah to sanctify the month based on testimony (matzvas kiddush al ha-re'iyah) independent of whether the calendar is dependent on it
An alternative reading suggesting that if Adar varies, we cannot avoid testifying and thus must permit Shabbat violation

topical_tags.term: batla Megillat Ta'anit (annulment of Megillat Ta'anit)
Rabbi Yochanan vs. Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi
Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua (Lod incident)
Five fast days (Tisha B'Av, Tzom Gedaliah, etc.)
Adar Rishon (first Adar in leap year)
Adar Sheni (second Adar in leap year)
